data.title: Nestlé Debt Tender Reshapes Balance Sheet As Shares Trade Below Targets
data.description:

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) Q3 2026 earnings snapshot

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) just posted Q3 2026 results with revenue of ¥284.8b and basic EPS of ¥7.03, as investors weigh these numbers against a forecast calling for revenue growth of 9.1% per year and earnings growth of about 19.6% per year. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between ¥236.5b and ¥284.8b over the past six reported periods, while basic EPS has ranged from ¥5.36 to ¥21.07, set against trailing twelve month EPS of ¥40.04 and net income of ¥92.8b. With net profit margins over the last year below the prior year, this earnings print puts the focus squarely on how quickly profitability can track those growth expectations.

¥16.3b net income sits against 9.1% margin trend

  • Q3 2026 net income excluding extra items was ¥16.3b on revenue of ¥284.8b, compared with a trailing twelve month net income of ¥92.8b on ¥1,016.7b of revenue, which lines up with the 9.1% net profit margin referenced for the last year.
  • Consensus narrative assumes earnings of ¥171.1b by 2028 with margins easing from 14.8% to 13.6%. The latest trailing margin at 9.1% and trailing twelve month net income of ¥92.8b show current profitability sitting well below those future margin and earnings levels.
    • In that balanced view, analysts are effectively looking for earnings to rise from ¥148.3b today to ¥171.1b even as margins soften, while the trailing margin data highlight that recent profitability has been tighter than the 14.8% starting point in the narrative.

  • Bulls point to forecast earnings growth of roughly 19.6% per year and bullish scenarios that look for earnings to reach ¥219.7b with EPS of ¥91.35 by 2028. The recent EPS pattern, including Q3 2026 EPS of ¥7.03 and a trailing EPS of ¥40.04, underlines how much of that growth still sits ahead rather than in the historical track record.
    • The bullish view expects profit margins to climb from 12.4% to 15.1%, while the trailing net margin referenced at 9.1% and the modest 0.4% annual EPS growth over five years give a much more restrained picture of realised earnings progress so far.
    • This earnings gap means the bullish case leans heavily on future benefits from AI, cloud and quick commerce investments compared with what the last twelve months of EPS and margins actually show.
Consistent mid teens earnings growth and higher margins are central to the optimistic story, so it is worth seeing how that compares with the detailed bull case for Alibaba Group Holding 🐂 Alibaba Group Holding Bull Case.

  • Bears focus on the risk that heavy AI and quick commerce spending, including the referenced multi year RMB 380b AI and cloud CapEx plan and earlier comments on free cash flow turning into a large outflow, keeps margins and earnings under pressure. That would make it harder for the stock to move closer to either the 196.78 target or the 249.17 DCF fair value despite those apparent gaps.
    • The recent drop in trailing net profit margin from 12.3% to 9.1% and the negative recent year on year earnings movement highlighted in the analysis both align with the cautious view that profitability has been under strain during this investment phase.
    • At the same time, the P/E of 20.7x sits only slightly above the North American Multiline Retail industry average of 20.4x and just below the peer average of 21.4x, which bears may see as leaving limited room for valuation expansion if margins do not improve from current levels.

  • Bulls argue that tech driven efficiency and higher renewal rates can support stronger profit trends, and the step up in net income and EPS across these halves aligns with that view, although:

The Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

data.title: Intuitive Machines (LUNR) Q4 Loss Of US$19.9 Million Tests Profitability Narrative
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (LUNR) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$44.8 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.17, alongside a net loss of US$19.9 million, while on a trailing 12 month basis revenue was US$210.1 million and EPS stood at a loss of US$0.71 with a net loss of US$83.9 million. Over recent quarters, the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$62.5 million in Q1 2025 to US$50.3 million in Q2, US$52.4 million in Q3 and US$44.8 million in Q4, with EPS losses ranging between US$0.06 and US$0.22 across the period.   • On a trailing 12 month basis, Intuitive Machines booked US$210.1 million in revenue against a net loss of US$83.9 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.71, which sits alongside five year losses that have grown at about 53.5% per year.
  • Critics highlight in the bearish narrative that persistent negative margins and heavy dependence on large government contracts could keep earnings under pressure, and the current loss profile lines up with that concern as quarterly net loss moved between US$7 million and US$25.3 million through FY 2025 while still relying heavily on mission and program awards to convert backlog into revenue.
    • The move from a Q4 2024 net loss of US$149.3 million to a Q4 2025 net loss of US$19.9 million shows that recent period results differ markedly from earlier TTM loss levels, which both bulls and bears will likely factor into their views on how quickly margins might improve.
    • Bears also point to the capital intensive model and customer concentration risk, and the trailing 12 month loss of US$83.9 million alongside ongoing investments highlighted in the narratives gives that risk a clear numerical anchor for anyone watching the path to profitability.
On this view, skeptics may feel recent earnings still leave little room for error before contract delays or cost overruns start to bite into already negative margins, and they may look to how management talks about funding and contract diversification in future updates before getting more comfortable with the long term earnings path.🐻 Intuitive Machines Bear Case

  • Forecasts in the data call for revenue growth of about 29.2% per year and earnings growth of about 55.77% per year with profitability expected within three years, yet the stock is currently trading on a P/S of 10.7x compared with peer and industry averages of 5.2x and 4.6x.

  • The stock has seen a 0.7% decline over the last week, a 10.4% decline over the past month, and a 1.5% decline year to date, while the 1 year, 3 year, and 5 year returns stand at a 1.9% decline, 34.7%, and 21.3% respectively.

Approach 1: NBT Bancorp Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank can earn above its cost of equity, then capitalizes those extra profits into an estimated per share value. It is less about raw earnings size and more about what those earnings represent relative to the equity invested.

For NBT Bancorp, the key inputs are:

  • Book Value: $36.32 per share
  • Stable EPS: $4.54 per share (source, weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 5 analysts)
  • Cost of Equity: $2.83 per share
  • Excess Return: $1.71 per share
  • Average Return on Equity: 11.18%
  • Stable Book Value: $40.56 per share (source, weighted future Book Value estimates from 6 analysts)

The model converts these excess returns into an intrinsic value of about $88.35 per share. Against a current price around $40.97, this implies a 53.6% discount, suggesting NBT Bancorp screens as materially undervalued on this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests NBT Bancorp is undervalued by 53.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 49 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for NBT Bancorp.

Approach 2: NBT Bancorp Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like NBT Bancorp, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay per share with what the bank earns per share. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a reasonable P/E often reflects two things: how quickly earnings are expected to change over time and how risky those earnings are perceived to be. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while more uncertainty or weaker growth expectations can justify a lower one.

NBT Bancorp currently trades on a P/E of 12.64x. That compares with an industry average P/E for Banks of 11.11x and a peer average of 14.36x, which puts NBT Bancorp between the broader sector and closer peers. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for NBT Bancorp is 13.34x, a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors such as earnings characteristics, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile. Because it adjusts for these elements rather than relying only on simple peer or industry comparisons, it can be a more tailored reference point. With the current P/E modestly below the Fair Ratio, the shares screen as slightly undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED
